📘 Maintainer Handbook

Latest · Community-maintained · Guide

Maintaining an open source project is rewarding but demanding. This handbook covers the practices, workflows, and mindset needed to sustain a healthy project long-term.

Issue Triage

Effective issue triage keeps your backlog manageable and contributors unblocked:

  • Respond to new issues within 48 hours, even if just to acknowledge
  • Use labels consistently: bug, enhancement, question, good first issue
  • Close stale issues with a polite explanation and a stale label
  • Pin high-priority issues and known bugs for visibility
  • Use issue templates to standardise bug reports and feature requests

Pull Request Reviews

Reviews are your primary quality gate. Make them efficient and welcoming:

  1. Acknowledge the PR quickly — even a "thanks, I'll review this week" helps
  2. Focus on correctness, security, and alignment with project goals
  3. Provide actionable feedback with concrete suggestions
  4. Approve when the PR is good enough — don't block on style preferences
  5. Merge promptly after approval to keep momentum
Use saved replies for common review comments. This speeds up reviews and ensures consistency across maintainers.

Release Management

Ship releases regularly to keep users engaged and reduce the risk of large, risky deployments:

  • Follow semantic versioning (SemVer) for predictable upgrades
  • Write clear release notes with migration guides for breaking changes
  • Automate releases with tools like Semantic Release or Release Please
  • Tag releases in git and publish to package registries
  • Maintain a CHANGELOG.md following Keep a Changelog format
# Example release workflow
git checkout main
git pull origin main
npm version minor -m "Release v%s"
git push origin main --tags
npm publish

Community Health

A healthy community is the foundation of a sustainable project:

  • Adopt and enforce a Code of Conduct (Contributor Covenant is widely used)
  • Recognise all contributions — code, docs, design, testing, mentoring
  • Create a CONTRIBUTORS.md or use All Contributors bot
  • Set clear expectations in CONTRIBUTING.md
  • Mentor new contributors with good first issue labels and pairing

Avoiding Burnout

Maintainer burnout is real. Protect yourself and your project:

  • Set boundaries — you don't owe anyone 24/7 support
  • Delegate — grow trusted contributors into co-maintainers
  • Automate — CI, issue bots, dependency updates, releases
  • Say no — not every feature request should be implemented
  • Take breaks — the project will survive a quiet week
If you're feeling overwhelmed, reach out. Many communities offer support for maintainers. It's okay to step back, find co-maintainers, or archive a project.

Security Practices

  • Enable Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency updates
  • Set up a SECURITY.md with responsible disclosure instructions
  • Use branch protection rules on main
  • Require signed commits for releases
  • Audit your supply chain — review new dependencies carefully

Project Documentation

Good documentation reduces support burden and attracts contributors:

  • README.md — Project overview, installation, quick start
  • CONTRIBUTING.md — How to contribute, development setup
  • CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md — Community standards
  • SECURITY.md — How to report vulnerabilities
  • CHANGELOG.md — Release history
  • LICENSE — Always include a clear license

© Open Source Guides · Resources · Community
